schema: The Deals sheet contains 47 deal rows (rows 2–48) with a header in row 1; columns A–G are pre-filled from a CRM export and column H (AuditFlag) is learner-computed. Three deals must be excluded: D-1023 has Status='Cancelled' (col G), D-1047 has Amount=0 (col E), and D-1046 has DealType='New Biz' (col D), which is a typo and not one of the three valid values ('New Business', 'Renewal', 'Expansion'). The SplitPct column (F) is 1.0 for sole-rep deals and 0.5 for the co-sell pair D-1031/D-1031B, which represent a single $40,000 contract split between R001 and R005 across two separate rows — a plain SUMIFS on Amount alone would count the full $40,000 for both reps, so revenue aggregation formulas must multiply Amount×SplitPct inside a SUMPRODUCT. The RepMaster sheet has 10 rows (rows 2–11); R006 'Jordan  Lee' contains a literal double-space between first and last name in column B, so TRIM() is required when retrieving any rep name or downstream text comparisons will return the raw double-spaced string. R010 Alex Chen was hired 2024-10-15, giving a pre-computed ProrationFactor of 17/31 ≈ 0.5484 in column G; all other reps have ProrationFactor=1.0, so the adjusted monthly quota formula must always multiply by ProrationFactor rather than treating it as optional. The CommissionRates sheet has NB tier rows in rows 2–5 with TierMin values 0, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0 sorted strictly ascending in column A — this ascending sort is required for VLOOKUP with approximate match (TRUE) to work correctly; the lookup returns the rate for the largest TierMin that is less than or equal to the attainment ratio. Standard and Accelerator plans use different rate columns (B and C respectively) for NB tiers, and separate flat rates apply for Renewal and Expansion deals by plan type (Standard Renewal 5%, Accelerator Renewal 7%, Standard Expansion 8%, Accelerator Expansion 10%). The Commission_Calculator sheet has columns A and L pre-filled and read-only; column L contains -1200 for R003 Jennifer Walsh and 0 for all other reps, and the commission cap of $25,000 from Settings!$B$2 applies after adding the clawback adjustment, meaning a rep whose BaseCommission+ClawbackAdj exceeds $25,000 is capped — R008 Michael Torres is the only rep who hits this cap.

T01.expectedBehavior: The formula must return the text string 'Exclude' (capital E) when ANY of three conditions is true: Status (col G) equals 'Cancelled', Amount (col E) equals 0, or DealType (col D) is not one of the three valid strings 'New Business', 'Renewal', or 'Expansion'. All other rows return 'Include' (capital I). Exactly three rows should return 'Exclude': the row for D-1023 (Cancelled), D-1046 (DealType='New Biz' typo), and D-1047 (Amount=0). The return type is always text — the formula must never return a blank, a Boolean, or a number; downstream SUMPRODUCT and COUNTIFS tasks filter on the literal string 'Include' and will silently return 0 if the flag values differ in case or spelling.

T02.expectedBehavior: The formula must return the rep's cleaned display name as a text string, with all runs of multiple internal spaces collapsed to single spaces by TRIM(). The critical case is R006, whose raw RepMaster value is 'Jordan  Lee' (two spaces); a formula without TRIM returns 'Jordan  Lee' with the double-space intact, which is visually wrong and would break any downstream text matching. All ten values must be plain first-last name text with no leading, trailing, or doubled spaces. XLOOKUP (or equivalent) must look up col A (RepID) in RepMaster col A and return col B, then TRIM must wrap the entire lookup result — applying TRIM only to the RepID key rather than the returned name will not fix the double-space.

T03.expectedBehavior: The formula must return the text string 'Standard' or 'Accelerator' for each rep by looking up RepID in RepMaster column A and retrieving column C (PlanType). No transformation or TRIM is needed for this column. The return values are case-sensitive inputs to downstream IF conditions in T10 and T11 — if the formula returns a value that doesn't exactly match 'Accelerator', those tasks will silently apply Standard rates to Accelerator reps. All ten rows must return text, never a number or blank.

T04.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a positive number representing (AnnualQuota / 12) × ProrationFactor for each rep. For the nine full-month reps the ProrationFactor is 1.0, so the result equals exactly AnnualQuota/12. For R010 Alex Chen the result must be AnnualQuota/12 × (17/31) ≈ 27419.35 — a formula that omits the ProrationFactor lookup or hard-codes 1.0 will return the full monthly quota of approximately 50000 for R010, which is wrong. Both the AnnualQuota (col D) and ProrationFactor (col G) must be fetched from RepMaster via lookup rather than hard-coded, as a single formula is expected to fill the entire range by dragging.

T05.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a non-negative number representing the sum of (Amount × SplitPct) for all deals where RepID matches, DealType is exactly 'New Business', and AuditFlag is exactly 'Include'. SUMPRODUCT is required (or an array-style formula) because it must multiply Amount by SplitPct element-wise before summing — a plain SUMIFS on Amount alone would return $40,000 for both R001 and R005 on the co-sell deal instead of the correct $20,000 each. A rep with no qualifying NB deals returns 0 (a number, not blank or text), which is the correct output. The formula must reference the AuditFlag column (Deals col H) computed in T01; if T01 is incorrect, T05 values will also be incorrect.

T06.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a non-negative number equal to the sum of (Amount × SplitPct) for all deals where RepID matches, DealType is exactly 'Renewal', and AuditFlag is 'Include'. The same SUMPRODUCT pattern used in T05 is required to correctly handle SplitPct weighting, even though no Renewal deal in this dataset happens to be split — using SUMIFS on Amount alone returns identical results here but is considered incorrect because it ignores SplitPct. A rep with no Renewal deals returns 0 as a number. All ten sample values are positive numbers; no rep has zero Renewal revenue in this dataset.

T07.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a non-negative number equal to the sum of (Amount × SplitPct) for all deals where RepID matches, DealType is exactly 'Expansion', and AuditFlag is 'Include'. R010 Alex Chen has no Expansion deals, so the correct return value is 0 (a number) — a learner should not interpret this as an error or attempt to substitute a blank or text. The same SUMPRODUCT pattern is required. All other nine reps return positive numbers ranging from 9000 to 20000 in the sample data.

T08.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a non-negative integer count of deals in the Deals sheet where RepID matches (col B) and AuditFlag is 'Include' (col H), regardless of DealType. COUNTIFS is the natural function here; SUMPRODUCT with a count pattern is also acceptable. The three excluded deals (D-1023, D-1046, D-1047) must not be counted for their respective reps — R003 Jennifer Walsh's count drops by one due to D-1023 being Cancelled. The co-sell deal rows D-1031 and D-1031B each count as one deal for their respective rep (R001 and R005), so they are not double-counted. The return type is always a non-negative integer; it must never return text or a decimal.

T09.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a decimal number (formatted as a percentage by the sheet) equal to NBRevenue (col E) divided by AdjMonthlyQuota (col D). The result is a raw decimal — for example 0.80 displays as 80% — and must not be multiplied by 100. This value is the direct input to the VLOOKUP tier lookup in T10, so precision matters: R010's attainment of approximately 0.649 must fall into the 50–80% tier (TierMin=0.5), and any rounding that pushes it to 0.65 or truncates it could still land in the same tier, but the formula itself should not apply any rounding. Division by zero is not expected in this dataset because all AdjMonthlyQuota values are positive numbers.

T10.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a decimal commission rate (e.g. 0.12 for 12%) looked up from the CommissionRates NB tier table (rows 2–5, columns A–C) using approximate match (VLOOKUP TRUE or equivalent), selecting column B for Standard plan reps and column C for Accelerator plan reps. The lookup value is QuotaAttainment (col I) and the lookup table must be CommissionRates!$A$2:$C$5 sorted ascending — using exact match (FALSE) will return #N/A for any attainment value that does not exactly equal a TierMin boundary. For example, R003's attainment of 0.95 falls in the 0.80–1.00 tier and must return 0.15 (Accelerator). A formula that looks up the rate without branching on PlanType will apply the same rate column to all reps and will return wrong values for all Accelerator reps.

T11.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a positive number equal to (NBRevenue × CommissionRate) + (RenewalRevenue × flatRenewalRate) + (ExpansionRevenue × flatExpansionRate), where flat rates differ by PlanType: Standard Renewal=0.05, Standard Expansion=0.08, Accelerator Renewal=0.07, Accelerator Expansion=0.10. The formula must branch on PlanType (col C) for the flat rates — a formula using a single flat rate for all reps will produce wrong results for Accelerator reps. R008 Michael Torres returns 25700 (before cap), which is the only value that exceeds the $25,000 cap; the cap is NOT applied in this column, only in T12. All return values are positive numbers; fractional cents are acceptable (e.g. R004 David Martinez returns 3692.5).

T12.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a number equal to MIN(BaseCommission + ClawbackAdj, 25000), referencing col K (BaseCommission) and the pre-filled col L (ClawbackAdj). R008 Michael Torres has BaseCommission=25700 and ClawbackAdj=0, so the result is capped at exactly 25000 — a formula without MIN returns 25700 for R008, which is wrong. R003 Jennifer Walsh has ClawbackAdj=-1200, reducing her result from 13525 to 12325, which is below the cap and is returned as-is. The cap value may be hard-coded as 25000 or referenced as Settings!$B$2; both are correct. The return type is always a number; the formula must never return text such as 'Capped' or a blank.
